forecasts.reasoning: The Trump administration and Defense Secretary Hegseth have explicitly directed the removal of climate-related content from Pentagon websites, viewing such initiatives as a distraction. The portal climate.mil has been offline since January 2025 and remains down as of May 2025. Given the administration's active purge of the word 'climate' and related programs—including the cancellation of 91 studies and the Navy's climate action plan—it is exceptionally unlikely they would restore a domain literally named 'climate.mil' within the 6 weeks between the knowledge cutoff (May 20, 2025) and resolution date (July 1, 2025). Even if internal tools for weather resilience are maintained, the specific public-facing domain will almost certainly remain offline.

The CDC currently recommends 0.7 mg/L. Although HHS Secretary RFK Jr. ordered a panel to review this recommendation in April 2025, bureaucratic processes for health panels (like the CPSTF) typically take many months or even years. The fact that the page has remained active as of May 20, over a month after the review was ordered, suggests standard protocol is being followed where existing guidance remains pending the new review. While there's a moderate risk of the page being taken offline or suspended before July 1 (a date just 41 days from the knowledge cutoff) given the aggressive anti-fluoride stance of the administration—such as the FDA announcement on May 13—the short 6-week timeframe heavily favors bureaucratic inertia. It is very likely the old recommendation will remain on the website until the panel formally completes its work.

Trump has been in office for roughly four months (past his first 100 days) and the search summary indicates he has not yet issued a presidential action using this exact phrase, despite issuing many early actions on immigration and DEI. With only about 40 days remaining until the July 1, 2025 deadline, the window for such an action is short. Furthermore, executive actions and proclamations often rely on precise statutory language (e.g., 'diversity immigrant' or 'INA 203(c)') rather than the colloquial 'Diversity Visa', though 'Diversity Visa Program' could be used. Given the short remaining timeline and the exact phrasing requirement, the probability is low.

The USDA's 'What Can SNAP Buy?' webpage has listed 'snack foods' as an eligible purchase category for years, and this language was still present as of May 5, 2025. Although the Trump administration (via HHS Secretary Kennedy and USDA Secretary Rollins) is currently encouraging states to submit waivers to ban junk food, candy, and soda from SNAP, these changes apply on a state-by-state basis and are still in the application/approval phase. Historically, federal agency webpage text updates lag behind high-level rhetoric unless there is an immediate, sweeping statutory change or a directed scrubbing effort. Having survived the initial 4 months of the administration's anti-junk food push, bureaucratic inertia and the reality that snack foods will remain federally eligible in most non-waiver states make it highly likely (around 71%) that the specific text will survive another 6 weeks until the July 1, 2025 resolution date. However, there is a moderate risk (29%) that an appointee reviews the page in the course of processing the state waivers and removes the "snack foods" example for optics reasons.

The forecast horizon is only ~41 days (May 20 to July 1, 2025). The Diversity Visa program is mandated by the Immigration Act of 1990, meaning the executive branch cannot unilaterally terminate it without congressional legislation. While the SAFE for America Act of 2025 aims to repeal the program, passing such legislation in a divided Senate (which requires 60 votes to overcome a filibuster) within a 41-day window is highly unlikely. Furthermore, as the Trump administration has been in office since January 20, 2025, any immediate executive attempts to maliciously remove the statutory webpages would likely have occurred during the initial 30-day agency review period. The instructions page is a stable URL that typically remains online year-round to provide information ahead of the October registration window. Given the short time-frame and the legal and administrative hurdles to dismantling the program or casually changing long-standing permanent URLs, the page is very likely to remain at its current location through July 1.
